coredata.dc:title: Evaluation of temperature-dependent mechanical properties of lithium-ion batteries: A hybrid experimental modal analysis and finite element method approach
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Soellner, Jonas
Berg, Philipp
Merkel, Matthias
Jossen, Andreas
coredata.dc:description:
The mechanical properties that influence the performance and durability of lithium-ion batteries under various operating conditions are crucial yet often overlooked. This work aims to deepen the understanding of these properties by developing a temperature-dependent orthotropic constitutive model. We present a detailed investigation of the mechanical responses of lithium-ion batteries, including Young’s and Shear Moduli, under various temperature conditions. To validate our findings, we have developed and implemented a robust finite element model that considers the elastic behavior and is based on experimental data from Experimental Modal Analysis of a lithium-ion pouch cell. This study unveils the significant temperature-dependent nature of these orthotropic mechanical properties. For instance, our model reveals a substantial reduction of the in plane Young’s Modulus ( E ) from 16 385.65MPa at 17.7°C to 6676.12MPa at 31.9°C. Our model’s parameters, particularly
E
X
and
E
Z
, display strong influence on the overall mechanical responses. We compare our results with published literature data which shows a good alignment with our findings. This study introduces a non-destructive approach to evaluate and quantify the mechanical parameters of lithium-ion pouch cells at the full cell level under various operating conditions. The insights derived from this study can inform the development of more efficient finite element models, thereby enhancing the understanding of mechanical properties of lithium-ion batteries.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of sensor placement on the lithium-ion pouch cell within a FEM framework. An isometric view of the pouch cell’s exterior, indicating the general dimensions and shape (left). Top view illustrating the grid layout with 25 designated locations for response sensors, specifically accelerometers labeled R11 to R33 (center). Bottom view detailing the placement of excitation locations and force sensor positions labeled E22 and E33 (right). The depicted sensor locations correspond to the sensor locations used in the EMA measurements published by Berg et al. in [25] that are used in our work. For detailed sensor placement and dimensions, refer to Fig. A.7 in the appendix.

Schematic representation of the research methodology: (A) Post-mortem cell measurements combined with an extensive literature review establish the foundation. (B) Extraction of initial mechanical parameter sets for simulation. (C) Sensitivity analysis in simulations aids in model optimization, capturing crucial parameter interdependencies. (D) Final phase includes temperature-dependent simulations leveraging experimental data from EMA measurements, yielding insights into parameter behavior under varying thermal conditions.

Sensitivity analysis of natural frequencies to orthotropic mechanical parameters: This figure illustrates the sensitivities of the model’s natural frequencies to changes in orthotropic YM, Poisson’s ratio, and Shear Modulus (SM). The plot demonstrates that individual natural frequencies are influenced by different sets of mechanical parameters, highlighting the importance of accurate material property assignments and consideration of orthotropic behavior in the simulation.

Comparative visualization of the first four mode shapes of the lithium-ion pouch cell from simulative and experimental modal analysis. Experimental results (top row) are depicted with wireframe models. Simulated results (middle row) show displacement magnitudes with color contours. Simulated Operational Deflection Shapes (ODS) at corresponding natural frequencies (bottom row) illustrate the cell’s operational response. Mode 1 (P33/P33) is a single-peak bending mode, Mode 2 (P22/P22) is a diagonal torsion mode, Mode 3 (P33/P33) shows a single-wave bending across the cell’s width, and Mode 4 (P22/P22) displays a symmetric wave-like bending. The simulated mode shapes correlate well with the experimental data.

Comparison of simulated and experimental natural frequencies for four different modes (1, 2, 3, and 4) at various temperatures ranging from 17.7°C to 31.9°C. The solid lines represent the simulated natural frequencies

head.ce:title: Evaluation of temperature-dependent mechanical properties of lithium-ion batteries: A hybrid experimental modal analysis and finite element method approach

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Electrical Energy Storage Technology, Technical University of Munich, Arcisstr. 21, 80333 Munich, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Electrical Energy Storage Technology, Technical University of Munich

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Electrical Energy Storage Technology, Technical University of Munich, Arcisstr. 21, 80333 Munich, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The mechanical properties that influence the performance and durability of lithium-ion batteries under various operating conditions are crucial yet often overlooked. This work aims to deepen the understanding of these properties by developing a temperature-dependent orthotropic constitutive model. We present a detailed investigation of the mechanical responses of lithium-ion batteries, including Young’s and Shear Moduli, under various temperature conditions. To validate our findings, we have developed and implemented a robust finite element model that considers the elastic behavior and is based on experimental data from Experimental Modal Analysis of a lithium-ion pouch cell. This study unveils the significant temperature-dependent nature of these orthotropic mechanical properties. For instance, our model reveals a substantial reduction of the in plane Young’s Modulus ( E ) from 16 385.65MPa at 17.7°C to 6676.12MPa at 31.9°C. Our model’s parameters, particularly
E
X
and
E
Z
, display strong influence on the overall mechanical responses. We compare our results with published literature data which shows a good alignment with our findings. This study introduces a non-destructive approach to evaluate and quantify the mechanical parameters of lithium-ion pouch cells at the full cell level under various operating conditions. The insights derived from this study can inform the development of more efficient finite element models, thereby enhancing the understanding of mechanical properties of lithium-ion batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries have firmly established themselves as the preferred energy storage solution for an extensive array of applications, spanning from handheld power tools and Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) to assorted consumer electronics [1–4]. For these mobile applications, a high energy density becomes a critical factor [5–7]. In pursuing this goal, cell and system design advancements continually strive for greater energy density and performance, prompting modifications in key design elements like electrode and separator thicknesses [8,9]. Such parameters significantly affect the structural dynamics of the battery system. Additionally, using batteries as structural components within broader systems has emerged as an innovative trend. Notably, auto manufacturers like Tesla have begun implementing battery packs that fulfill a dual purpose, leveraging their mechanical robustness to contribute to the rigidity of the overall battery pack construction. This dual role underscores the imperative for a comprehensive understanding of the mechanical properties intrinsic to battery cells [10].

In the quest for this understanding, Sahraei et al. performed extensive mechanical tests on pouch and cylindrical lithium-ion cells under several loading conditions. Their study has revealed that the structural integrity of lithium-ion cells is subject to deformation and failure under certain mechanical stress conditions, indicating the importance of understanding these dynamics for the safe and efficient design of battery cells [11]. They demonstrated that mechanical behavior greatly influences lithium-ion batteries’ performance and safety, a factor often overlooked in the design process. These insights set the foundation for the need to investigate and quantify the mechanical responses of battery cells under various conditions, which is one of the objectives of our study. The mechanical behavior of lithium-ion batteries is crucial to maintaining their structural robustness, especially when faced with challenging operational conditions such as high temperatures, significant mechanical stress, or during accident scenarios [12–14]. Notably, these mechanical attributes can directly affect the electrochemical performance of the batteries, influencing aspects like internal stress distribution, electrolyte movement, and electrode deformation [15,16]. Therefore, an in-depth understanding of these mechanical characteristics is essential for optimizing battery performance, enhancing safety measures, and extending their operational lifespan. Li and Zhu developed a large deformation and fracture model for lithium-ion battery cells treated as a homogenized medium. The study proposed an anisotropic plasticity model that includes State of Charge (SoC) and rate-dependence of plasticity and crack initiation, calibrated and validated using experimental data on large-format pouch batteries. This model effectively characterizes the mechanical behavior of the cell under various external loads, providing insights into the internal short circuit mechanisms triggered by mechanical deformation [17]. Li et al. conducted a study to investigate the mechanical damage and Internal Short Circuit (ISC) behavior of prismatic lithium-ion cells under bending loads, employing three-point bending tests, X-ray CT scans, and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) to understand the deformation, fracture patterns, and electrochemical performance changes.

Understanding the mechanical properties of lithium-ion batteries under various temperatures is crucial for optimizing their design to enhance durability and performance across different operating conditions. This study enables engineers to evaluate mechanical properties for different temperatures in a non-destructive way, which is challenging with many other methods. The parameters derived from this study can help refine mechanical models, providing a more accurate parameter set for simulations. This advancement aids in the development of a more robust and reliable understanding of lithium-ion batteries. Additionally, this knowledge contributes to the advancement of finite element models, allowing for more accurate predictions of battery behavior under different thermal conditions. The findings from this study have the potential to significantly impact the development of future lithium-ion batteries, making them safer, more efficient, and better suited for a wide range of applications, from electric vehicles to portable electronics.

Having emphasized the significance of a comprehensive knowledge of lithium-ion batteries’ mechanical properties, assessing the current state-of-the-art in this realm becomes crucial. Numerous researchers have employed varied approaches to understanding these systems’ structural dynamics, each contributing substantially to the existing literature and incrementally refining our collective knowledge [12–16,18–20]. Recently, [21] developed a detailed finite element model for predicting separator damage in a Li-ion pouch cell exposed to transverse compression. This approach combines in-plane and out-of-plane behavior modeling to balance computational efficiency and physical accuracy. Their model successfully predicts internal short circuits based on separator failure, providing valuable insights for enhancing battery design and safety [21]. [22] investigated the out-of-plane deformation and failure behavior of a 41 Ah NMC pouch cell under varying loading rates and orientations, revealing significant direction-dependent behavior at low-speed loading and increased stiffness and reduced failure strain at high-speed loading due to viscosity effects of the liquid electrolyte [22]. This study underscores the importance of considering direction and strain rate dependencies in crash safety evaluations of lithium-ion pouch cells.

Several published studies used Experimental Modal Analysis (EMA) to characterize the structural response of Lithium-Ion Battery (LIB). [23] investigated the structural frequency response of LIB using impact hammer and shaker excitation tests. The boundary conditions for impact testing are not specified in the study, whereas they performed shaker testing with a clamped boundary condition. This study did not report temperature values for either excitation method.

Popp et al. investigated the structural response of LIB pouch cells using a miniature shaker with an impedance head with a low excitation amplitude. They found that using a non-destructive method called the Frequency Response Function (FRF), it is possible to monitor changes in the battery’s internal structure and degradation over time. The study also showed that low amplitude forces and low-frequency regions could be used to detect differences in the SoC and State of Health (SoH), as well as mechanical properties and how they are affected by temperature. However, the authors also noted that the FRF is not specific enough to determine a single parameter without prior knowledge of the system [18].

Galos et al. studied the effect of embedding LIB in carbon fiber laminates and sandwich panels on their vibration and acoustic properties. The study found that placing batteries at nodal points increases the vibration-damping ratio for specific modes. The results indicate that the placement of LIB can improve the vibration-damping properties of carbon fiber laminates and sandwich composites [19].

[20] studied the natural frequencies and mode shapes of a commercially available 25 Ah Nickel Manganese Cobalt Oxide (NMC) laminate pouch cell using the impulse excitation technique. The study aimed to better understand key system attributes such as natural frequencies, damping, and mode shapes of battery cells under mechanical load to minimize the impact of vibration-induced resonance in future electric and hybrid electric vehicle batteries. The study was conducted on 15 cells at five different values of state of charge SoC. The results showed that the same four modes of vibration existed within a frequency range of
191 − 360
Hz
, which is above the frequency range typically associated with road-induced vibration [24]. Additionally, the results indicated that the cell’s natural frequencies and damping did not vary with changing values of SoC.

In a two-part study, Berg et al. conducted a comprehensive investigation into the structural dynamics of lithium-ion batteries under vibrational loadings [25,26]. In the first part, Berg et al. focused on optimizing the measurement quality and uncovering the influence of different cell parameters [25]. Through experimental modal analysis, they examined various cell parameters, such as state of charge, state of health, temperature, and excitation amplitude. They discovered significant sensitivity in structural response from the excitation amplitude due to nonlinear softening and damping. They also found considerable softening with increasing temperature, attributed to changes in the viscoelasticity of the separator. The study revealed minor sensitivity to the state of charge, while cyclic aging significantly increased cell stiffness. The acquired results are helpful for Finite Element Method (FEM) building and assessing the potential fatigue during vibration testing. In the second part, Berg et al. used their test bench to conduct the first experimental investigation of the structural dynamics of large-format prismatic cells typically found in battery electric vehicles, marking the first experimental investigation of its kind [26]. They found notable differences in the sensitivity of the natural frequencies on the state of charge compared to their previous pouch cell investigations, attributing this variation to the different boundary conditions established by the cell casing. Additionally, they observed a linear characteristic in the temperature sensitivity, echoing findings from the first study, and detected displacement of the jelly roll inside new cells due to an initially present gap. The study concluded that vibrational load, particularly for new and unconstrained cells, could potentially induce stressful conditions for the jelly rolls. Researchers can use the findings from this study to design vibration abuse experiments aiming at maximum stress, and to enhance their understanding of vibration’s potential impact on the lifespan and safety of these cells.

Understanding the mechanical behavior of lithium-ion batteries under different loading conditions has been a significant research area in simulation-based mechanical research. For instance, Sahraei et al. delivered an in-depth study involving extensive mechanical tests on lithium-ion cells under various loading conditions, providing vital insights into the deformation and failure mechanisms of these cells [11]. Raffler et al. investigated the out-of-plane deformation and failure behavior of a 41 Ah NMC pouch cell under varying loading rates and orientations, revealing significant direction-dependent behavior at low-speed loading and increased stiffness and reduced failure strain at high-speed loading due to viscosity effects of the liquid electrolyte Raffler et al.. This study underscores the importance of considering direction and strain rate dependencies in crash safety evaluations of lithium-ion pouch cells.

Ali et al. focused on computational models for LIB cells and their behavior under in-plane constrained compression tests [27]. They further extended this work by adopting macro homogenized material models to simulate an in-plane constrained punch indentation test of a small-scale LIB module specimen [28]. Sahraei et al. expanded the scope of testing by including both local and global compression scenarios, examining three types of lithium-ion pouch cells [29]. An alternate perspective was provided by Sangrós Giménez et al., who employed a Discrete Element Method (DEM) to simulate the microstructural and mechanical properties of LIB electrodes, emphasizing the importance of electrode structural stability [12]. Tang et al. proposed a general method to establish a computational homogenized model for the cylindrical battery module, focusing on Electric Vehicle (EV) crash safety numerical simulation [30]. Their goal was to reduce model complexity and hence computation time. This notion of creating scalable models was also echoed by Beaumont et al., who developed a macro Finite Element (FE) model of a pouch cell that can be applied to a range of chemistries, external geometries, and internal cell constructions [31]. Furthermore, Keshavarzi et al. focused on the deformation and failure mechanisms of lithium-ion batteries under complex practical loadings, offering an analytical characterization method to extract homogenized tensile properties of a cell from in-situ bending tests [32]. Along the same lines, Gilaki et al. presented a comprehensive framework for optimizing LIB pack design through an iterative process of homogenization, FE simulation, and submodeling [33].

In recent years, FEM based studies have become increasingly prevalent, rigorously investigating the structural dynamics of lithium-ion pouch cells [34–36]. These investigations often include using EMA to calibrate and validate their models, forming our study’s focal point. We utilize EMA data published by Berg et al. in [25] to optimize the model parameters, initiating the process with literature-based values for elastic parameters. We achieve a robust match with the EMA results through meticulous optimization, subsequently exploring the model’s sensitivity to varying material parameters. The resultant elastic parameters, obtained through our iterative process, are then compared with those established in the literature. With this EMA-based parameterization approach, we intend to enhance the accuracy of simulations concerning the behavior of lithium-ion pouch cells under dynamic loads, thus informing and refining their design and optimization in a multitude of applications.

The literature provides an interesting array of Young’s Modulus (YM) ( E ) and Poisson’s ratio ( ν ) values for different lithium-ion cell components, showcasing the complexity of mechanical characterizations in such heterogeneous systems. For instance, [27] examined both the compressive and tensile moduli for an anode with a copper current collector foil, reporting values of
188
MPa
and
4700
MPa
, respectively. The vast discrepancy between these two measures emphasizes the strong dependency of mechanical properties on the applied load type, which has been a key consideration in our model’s development. The separator, a crucial component influencing lithium-ion cells’ mechanical behavior, was the study subject for [27] and [37]. The YM values reported varied significantly, depending on the type of mechanical load (tensile or compressive) and the test conditions (dry or wet). These findings underscore the need to account for varying environmental and operational conditions when characterizing mechanical properties, a feature that our model is designed to incorporate. Several studies [11,38–40] also provided homogenized or effective YM values for pouch cells. These values, typically lower than those reported for individual components, illustrate the significant impact of cell microstructure and component interactions on the overall mechanical behavior. The reported Poisson’s ratios varied from as low as 0.01 [11,41] to as high as 0.3 [42], demonstrating the considerable range of this parameter depending on the specific cell design and structure. The provided literature values underscore the intricate interplay of mechanical properties within a lithium-ion cell and the crucial influence of factors such as load type, environmental conditions, and cell design.

When analyzing the mechanical behavior of a lithium-ion battery as a whole, it is important to consider how the individual YMs different components interact and contribute to the overall mechanical properties of the battery. A lithium-ion cell is a composite structure consisting of multiple layers and materials, each with distinct mechanical properties. In a lithium-ion cell, cathode and anode active materials, binders, current collectors, and separators all contribute to the overall mechanical behavior of the battery. The mechanical properties of the cell can be considered as an effective or equivalent value that takes into account the combination of the mechanical properties of the individual components. The overall effective YM of a lithium-ion cell can be estimated by combining the individual YM of its components using various approaches, such as the Voigt average technique [43], the Rule of Mixture (ROM) or micromechanical models. However, this estimation can be quite complex due to the heterogeneity of the materials involved and their different contributions to the mechanical behavior of the cell. The ROM is a simplified approach that can be used to estimate the overall YM of a composite material. It considers the volume fraction of each component in the composite and the YM of the individual materials. For a lithium-ion cell, the effective YM (
E
e f f e c t i v e
) can be calculated as:
(1)
E
e f f e c t i v e
=
∑
i
E
i
×
V
i
where
E
i
is the Young’s Modulus of an individual component ( i ), and
V
i
is the volume fraction of that component in the cell. However, this approach provides only a rough estimation of the overall Young’s Modulus and may not capture the true mechanical behavior of the lithium-ion cell, especially if the interactions between the components or the anisotropic nature of some materials are not taken into account. More advanced micromechanical models or numerical simulations (e.g., FEM) can be employed to obtain a more accurate estimation of the overall mechanical properties of a lithium-ion cell. Given the wide range of Young’s Modulus values for individual components, the effective Young’s Modulus of a lithium-ion cell can also vary significantly, depending on the specific materials and their volume fractions within the cell.

In this work, we present an approach rooted in the mechanical characterization of lithium-ion cells through EMA, drawing from previously published results for small-format pouch cells [25] and large-format prismatic cells [26]. These foundational studies revealed highly nonlinear dynamic responses for both cell types, and significantly, the strong influence of the cell casing on the cell’s response, alongside respective sensitivities to the SoC, cell temperature, and aging. Building on these insights, we introduce a FEM model of a lithium-ion pouch cell, initializing with literature-derived elastic parameters. The model is then meticulously optimized to match EMA results, thus ensuring a high degree of model-experimental data congruence. This process allows us to evaluate the sensitivity of various elastic parameters and to estimate anisotropic mechanical parameters that align with EMA measurements at different temperatures. Our approach distinguishes itself from previous FEM-based studies, such as those by [36] and [35], through its explicit consideration of temperature variance on evaluated mechanical parameters, thereby enhancing the applicability of our results across a broader range of operating conditions. While Sonnberger et al. provided a robust basis for modal analysis and parameter derivation, their focus remained predominantly on SoC-dependent material properties.

The data obtained from EMA, integrated with FEM-based simulations, is employed for model parameterization in this publication, resulting in a more accurate depiction of the battery’s mechanical behavior. By assessing the temperature dependency of mechanical parameters and applying our methodology to various datasets of experimental data, we ensure a low error margin between the model and measured data, thereby validating the applicability of our approach under diverse operational circumstances. Our work thus advances previous studies in the field, offering a more comprehensive method for evaluating the mechanical parameters of lithium-ion batteries across varying temperatures.

The simulations presented in this study are grounded in experimental data from previous work by Berg et al. in [25], utilizing Kokam SLPB356495 cells with a capacity of 2.1Ah, featuring a NMC cathode and a graphite anode. To ensure accuracy, the geometry of each cell was meticulously measured with a digital caliper, repeating each dimension five times and computing the arithmetic mean for use as the standard value. The cell stack composition includes seven double side coated cathode and eight double side coated anode sheets, with the outermost cathode sheets being single side coated and a Z-folded separator.

For our FEM simulations, mechanical parameters such as YM and Poisson’s ratio were initially sourced from literature, as detailed in Table 1. These parameters served as the starting point for our model, guiding the initial setup before fine-tuning through optimization. The initial parameter set used for the fitting procedure is shown in Table 2, which includes values for
E
X
,
E
Y
,
E
Z
, shear moduli
G
X Y
,
G
Y Z
,
G
X Z
, and Poisson’s ratios
ν
X Y
,
ν
Y Z
,
ν
X Z
. This initial set was chosen based on a combination of literature values, experimental insights, and theoretical considerations, ensuring a realistic and relevant foundation for our model. The optimization process involved adjusting these parameters and evaluating their sensitivities to accurately reflect the lithium-ion pouch cell’s mechanical behavior, with a focus on aligning our model closely with the EMA findings. This approach not only ensured the model’s accuracy but also provided a reliable representation of the cell’s mechanical behavior under various operational conditions.

A mesh was created for the cell geometry using the ANSYS Meshing tool. The mesh density was varied to study its effect on the accuracy of the simulation results. The optimized mesh used for the simulations is listed in B.5. The cell was assumed to be supported on all sides, allowing for free–free boundary conditions. This is in alignment with the experimental setup that was used in the study published by Berg et al. in [25]. We used the experimental data from this study as a basis for our fitting procedure. The experimental setup comprised a shaker, a data acquisition system, force transducers, accelerometers, and a custom-built chamber with temperature control capabilities. Temperature regulation within the chamber was achieved using a Peltier element. However, due to the chamber’s limited thermal insulation and the Peltier element’s modest cooling capacity, the achievable temperature range was constrained. Consequently, the temperature range represented in the published data is relatively narrow, extending from 17.7°C to 31.9°C. The primary focus of this simulation is on modes 1 to 4, which aligns with the lower frequency range where the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) is superior. Consequently, the modeling effort is strategically confined to the analysis of the first four modes. The frequency range of the modal analysis hence was set to a range of 0 to
1400
Hz
to cover the expected natural frequencies of the cell.

For the present work we chose ANSYS Workbench 2020 R1 as a simulation environment. The software provides a FEM based simulation module for modal analysis. This simulation module provides different blocks for mode shape and resonance frequency analysis as well as harmonic response analysis. The geometry required for our simulation was derived from an approximation of the cell geometry. Fig. 1 shows a schematic representation of the cell that was used in the work of by Berg et al. in [25] additional relevant parameters are displayed in Table 3. This approximation was constructed using the dimensions garnered from the measurements described earlier. For this purpose, a Computer Aided Design (CAD) software named Spaceclaim, a component of the ANSYS product suite, was employed. Using these dimensions, an approximate geometry of the cell was designed within the CAD environment of Spaceclaim. The developed geometry was then integrated into the ANSYS Workbench 2020 R1 simulation platform for subsequent analysis.

In the initial stages of our study, we explored a modeling approach based on isotropic mechanical parameters. However, it became evident that this isotropic model was not sufficient for accurately reflecting the results obtained from EMA. This limitation stems from the nature of isotropic models, where alterations in material parameters impact both bending and torsion modes uniformly, which does not accurately represent the complex behavior of lithium-ion pouch cells. Recognizing this discrepancy, we shifted our focus to a more sophisticated and precise approach. We adopted a homogeneous model with orthotropic material properties, which allowed us to differentiate between the mechanical responses of bending and torsion modes more effectively. This transition to an orthotropic model was a critical step in enhancing the accuracy of our simulations, ensuring they are more closely aligned with the empirical findings observed in EMA.
Fig. 2 depicts the modeling and optimization approach that we followed, which consists of four primary phases:

Fig. 2 a: Post-mortem Cell Measurements and Literature Review - The initial phase involves comprehensive post-mortem cell measurements and an extensive review of the existing literature. This step establishes the foundation for our study by providing initial values for the mechanical parameters and insights into the cell’s composition and behavior.

Fig. 2 b: Extraction of Initial Mechanical Parameter Sets - In this phase, we extract the initial mechanical parameter set, including Young’s Modulus (

). These parameters are sourced from literature and are displayed in 2.

Fig. 2 c: Sensitivity Analysis and Model Optimization - The extracted parameters serve as the starting point for the sensitivity analysis. In this process, the initial values are systematically varied within a predefined range, and the corresponding changes in the natural frequencies (
f
n a t
) are observed. This analysis provides insights into the dependencies of individual model parameters on their influence on specific or multiple vibration modes. By including this step, the subsequent temperature-dependent fitting can be conducted in a more informed and precise manner.

Fig. 2 d: Temperature-Dependent Model Fitting - The final phase involves fitting the model to the results using experimental data from EMA measurements. This step provides detailed insights into how the mechanical parameters behave under varying thermal conditions. By systematically adjusting the model to match the experimental data at different temperatures, we obtain a comprehensive temperature-dependent parameter set. X Y
,
ν
Y Z
,
ν
X Z
) that accurately reflect the behavior of the lithium-ion pouch cells under the studied conditions.

The modeling and simulation work used a curve-fitting method of the FRF on the points
E
33
/
R
33
and
E
22
/
R
22
from the harmonic response analysis in ANSYS. These points represent sensor locations on the cell surfaces
E
X X
for excitation force measurement and
R
X X
for response acceleration measurement (compare Fig. 1). The evaluation criteria for the modeling and simulation included natural frequencies, mode shapes, mode sequence, and frequency response functions on
E
33
/
R
33
and
E
22
/
R
22
. The primary optimization criterion in our study is the minimization of error in simulated to experimentally determined natural frequencies
f
n a t , n
for individual modes, as observed between simulation and experimental data. This can be formulated as:
(2)
Minimize
Δ
f
sim-exp
=
∑
n = 1
N
|
f
nat , n
sim
−
f
nat , n
exp
|
where
Δ
f
sim-exp
denotes the total absolute difference in natural frequencies between simulation and experiment for the n -th mode,
f
nat , n
sim
represents the simulated natural frequency for the n -th mode,
f
nat , n
exp
is the experimental natural frequency for the n -th mode. After defining the general optimization framework, we aimed to find a parameter set for the orthotropic model in which the orthotropic behavior of YM was less prominent. This intention was based on the fact that gravimetrically and volumetrically dominating active cell components, such as the electrodes, show a low rate of orthotropy regarding YM measured via a tensile test [12,27,46,47], compared to passive components like the separator [23,39,48–55]. After applying the rule of mixtures, as defined in Eq. (1), which considers the volumetric portion of the components, the orthotropy of the homogeneous material parameters was not expected to be as prominent as for the microporous separator. However, this approach did not yield sufficient results from the SMA in ANSYS.

We achieved the best-fit model with a parameter set where the orthotropic behavior of the homogeneous material was more similar to that of the microporous separator. This model most accurately reflected the results from the EMA of the pouch cell at 24.9°C surface temperature. To create an optimized, temperature-responsive model, we formulated several strategic rules and assumptions, establishing a semi-physics-based approach. These guidelines included maintaining a constant pattern in parameter changes and ensuring these changes were linear or near-linear. This approach ensured that parameter variations triggered by temperature shifts consistently aligned with the observed behavioral trends of the pouch cell within the temperature range under investigation. For example, the behavior of
E
X
– which exhibited a continuous increase inversely proportional to the temperature decrease – illustrates this approach. Consistent with the enhanced stiffness of the pouch cell as evidenced by the experimental data in [25], this trend persisted without exception, preventing any transitory dips in temperature that could artificially enhance matching results or FRF outcomes. Given the predominantly linear temperature-dependent behavior – alternating between softening and stiffening – our model mirrored this linearity in its parameters. As the pouch cell’s stiffness increased with decreasing temperature, it necessitated a corresponding rise in the elastic material parameters. Conversely, with increasing temperature leading to a softening of the pouch cell, the elastic material parameters decreased accordingly. The Poisson’s ratio was held constant at 0.01 because its effect on the simulated
f
n a t
was negligible, and this value is commonly used in other studies [11,39,41,42]. Our approach can be best described as semi-physics-based, combining empirical observations with key physical principles while recognizing the need for simplifications in the model. To further support the validity of our optimized model, we have included a detailed error analysis and presented both the initial and optimized parameter sets in the following sections. This comprehensive modeling approach, incorporating both orthotropic material behavior and temperature-dependent parameters, offers valuable insights into the behavior of pouch cells under varying conditions. This simulation resulted in six temperature-dependent parameters (
E
X
,
E
Y
,
E
Z
,
G
X Y
,
G
Y Z
,
G
X Z
), representing the best possible temperature behavior of the pouch cell within the assumptions mentioned above. The modal damping ratios of modes 1 to 4, modeled with Modal Damping (MDAMP), were set according to the results of the EMA and compared with the experimental damping ratios. Temperature-dependent optimization rules were established to maintain a consistent regime of parameter changes, linear or near-linear parameter changes, and a constant Poisson ratio of 0.01. The resulting temperature-dependent model consisted of six parameters and accurately represented the temperature behavior of the pouch cell while adhering to established rules and assumptions. This comprehensive modeling approach, incorporating both orthotropic material behavior and temperature-dependent parameters, offers valuable insights into the behavior of pouch cells under varying conditions.
This section discusses the resulting mechanical parameters of our modeling approach. We also discuss how our results compare to values previously reported in the literature.

The adjustment of the nine elastic material parameters reveals distinct sensitivities of the individual modes to different single material parameters. A key observation from Fig. 3 is that
E
X
which is the YM in the X-direction, as defined in Fig. 1 predominantly influences mode 3. In contrast,
E
Y
does not have a significant impact on any of the modes. This difference underscores that the orientation and arrangement of the cell, especially as visualized in Fig. 1, play a pivotal role in how these material parameters affect the natural frequencies. Furthermore, this finding implies that
E
Y
cannot be effectively evaluated through this method since its value does not influence the simulated natural frequencies.
E
Z
which is the YM in the Z-direction (
E
Z
) influences modes 1 and 4, but with varying degrees of significance. The influence of
E
Z
follows a descending order, with mode 1 being more significantly impacted than mode 4. In terms of Poisson’s ratios (
ν
X Y
,
ν
Y Z
, and
ν
X Z
), none of them exhibit a significant influence on the simulated natural frequencies, which also indicates, that they cannot be evaluated via this technique.

Finally, the SM in the XY plane (
G
X Y
) also shows no notable influence on the modes, and as such, it shares a similar conclusion with
E
Y
and Poisson’s ratios. However,
G
Y Z
displays a similar level of influence on all modes except for mode 3. Meanwhile,
G
X Z
uniquely influences mode 2. These observations emphasize the importance of accurately identifying and assigning elastic material parameters to understand their effects on the behavior of the different modes, as well as to optimize the simulation process. The findings from the sensitivity analysis offer valuable insights into the evaluation of mechanical parameters when fitting the model to previously experimentally measured modal analysis data. Since different modes are influenced by various sets of material parameters, it is possible to identify and isolate the most influential parameters for each mode. This knowledge can significantly improve the model fitting process by focusing on optimizing the most relevant parameters, leading to a more accurate representation of the actual cell behavior. Considering the mode shapes during the optimization process is of utmost importance. While optimizing the model using an automated approach, the optimization typically focuses on matching the value of the simulated natural frequency. However, it is crucial to ensure that the model is not only fitted to match the natural frequencies but also takes into account the respective mode shapes. This is because mode shapes provide a comprehensive understanding of the deformation patterns and vibrational characteristics of the lithium-ion pouch cell, which can be linked to its overall performance and failure mechanisms. By closely examining the mode shapes and considering them during the optimization process, researchers can gain insights into the cell’s mechanical behavior and its response to various loading conditions. This knowledge is essential for understanding the cell’s structural integrity, potential failure points, and developing effective strategies to improve its performance and durability. Automating this optimization process is challenging because it requires the simultaneous consideration of both natural frequencies and mode shapes, which adds complexity to the algorithm and demands more computational resources. The optimization process was predominantly conducted manually, which facilitated a more precise representation of the real-world behavior of the lithium-ion pouch cell. This approach incorporated considerations of both natural frequencies and mode shapes. As detailed in Section 2, the objective of the optimization was to minimize deviations in
f
n a t
, while concurrently maintaining the experimentally determined mode sequence. This was achieved through visual verification of the mode shapes. Incorporating mode shapes into the optimization process, either through manual or automated approaches, is crucial for the development of accurate and reliable models. By considering both natural frequencies and mode shapes, we can gain a more comprehensive understanding of the cell’s behavior, which can ultimately lead to improved quality of the parameter evaluation. Moreover, the identification of material parameters with minimal or no impact on the simulated natural frequencies, such as
E
Y
and
ν
X Y
,
ν
Y Z
and
ν
X Z
, can help streamline the fitting process. By excluding these parameters from the optimization process or assigning lower priority, computational resources can be conserved, and the efficiency of the parameter evaluation can be increased. Additionally, understanding the sensitivity of each mode to specific material parameters (compare Fig. 3) allows for the possibility of targeted optimization. However, the findings from the sensitivity analysis also reveal certain limitations of the method due to the lack of sensitivity to specific material parameters. As observed, parameters
E
Y
,
ν
X Y
,
ν
Y Z
and
ν
X Z
, and
G
X Y
show minimal or no significant impact on the simulated natural frequencies. This lack of sensitivity presents a challenge when trying to accurately evaluate these parameters using this particular method. Since these parameters do not notably influence the simulation results, it is not possible to determine their true values via this technique. While the current model does not significantly emphasize the less-sensitive parameters, they highlight areas for detailed examination in subsequent research. These parameters, although not dominant in the modal analysis, may play crucial roles in other aspects of the cell’s performance. Expanding the model or using alternative methods in conjunction with supplementary experimental data, such as mechanical or thermal measurements, could offer a more holistic evaluation of these parameters in future studies. For example in-situ tensile test as demonstrated by Keshavarzi et al. in [32] could add valuable insights into the through-plane modulus of the cells. Combining the two methods could provide a comprehensive evaluation of the mechanical properties, thereby enhancing the robustness and reliability of the model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Natural frequencies and mode shapes of the optimized model
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the sensitivity analysis, we used the results to adjust the model parameters to fit the measured structural response. Fig. 4 shows a comparison between the simulated and experimentally determined mode shapes of the investigated pouch cell. Additionally, the simulated ODS are included to illustrate the cell’s response under operational conditions at the corresponding natural frequencies. The ODS provides enhanced visualization of the cell’s dynamic behavior, depicting how it deforms under real-world operational conditions. This additional layer of analysis helps to validate the experimental data and offers a more intuitive understanding of the dynamic response. The inclusion of ODS is particularly beneficial for structures with high modal density and significant damping ratios, where discerning individual modes can be challenging. The complex interactions between modes are better captured in the ODS, helping to explain discrepancies between the measured and simulated mode shapes, notably for mode 3. Overall, simulative modal analysis, complemented by ODS, provides valuable insights and improves the accuracy of results in the EMA of lithium-ion cells.

Fig. 5 provides a comprehensive comparison between the simulated and experimental natural frequencies for each of the four modes across a range of temperatures. Upon close examination of the data, several interesting observations can be made. For mode 1, the simulated and experimental natural frequencies are in close agreement across the entire temperature range. The differences between simulated and experimental values are relatively small, with a maximum deviation of 1.65Hz and a minimum deviation of 0.18Hz. This indicates that the simulation model accurately captures the behavior of mode 1 across the considered temperature range. For mode 2, the simulated natural frequencies follow the same trend as the experimental data. However, the deviations between the simulated and experimental values are slightly larger than those observed for mode 1. The maximum deviation reaches 6.30Hz, while the minimum deviation is 0.7Hz. Despite these deviations, the overall agreement between the simulated and experimental data for mode 2 is still reasonably good. Mode 3 shows a similar trend as modes 1 and 2, with the simulated natural frequencies closely following the experimental data.

The deviations between the simulated and experimental values range from 0.1Hz to 2.57Hz, indicating a good level of agreement between the two sets of data. For mode 4, the simulated natural frequencies exhibit larger variances when compared to the experimental values. These deviations are more significant for mode 4 than for the other modes. However, it is essential to consider that these substantial deviations may not purely indicate the model’s limitations. It could also be the case that the quality of measurement or the fitting procedure for these higher-order modes may have deteriorated, thereby affecting the accuracy of the experimental ground truth. Prior studies, such as those by Berg et al., have reported less reproducible results for higher-order modes under certain conditions, suggesting potential inconsistencies in the measurement quality at these frequencies [25]. A more detailed analysis of the deviations is presented in Table 4. The mean deviations for modes 1, 2, and 3 are 0.79Hz, 2.01Hz, and 0.79Hz, respectively, while the median deviations are 0.5Hz, 1.7Hz, and 0.5Hz. This indicates a good agreement between the simulated and experimental values for these modes. The relative deviations are also quite low, ranging from 0.01% to 0.46% for the minimum and maximum values, respectively. However, the deviations for mode 4 are more significant, with a mean deviation of 60.48Hz and a median deviation of 48.5Hz. The relative deviations for this mode range from 0.41% to 11.31%. The Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) values for modes 1, 2, and 3 are 1.13Hz, 3.57Hz, and 2.46Hz, respectively. While the comparison between simulated and experimental natural frequencies suggests that the simulation model is reasonably accurate for modes 1, 2, and 3, it highlights the complexities of accurately predicting mode 4, potentially requiring both a refinement of the model and a consideration of the quality of the experimental measurements at these higher modes.

Fig. 6 shows the evaluated values of the mechanical parameters as functions of temperature. The YM in the X and Z directions (
E
X
,
E
Z
) are shown in panel a. At 17.7°C,
E
X
and
E
Z
were evaluated to be approximately 3225.49MPa and 16 385.65MPa, respectively. As the temperature increased to 31.9°C,
E
X
and
E
Z
decreased to about 1828.49MPa and 6676.12MPa, respectively. Notably,
E
Z
showed the most significant change, with a decrease of almost 60% over the temperature range.

Panel b shows the evaluated values for the SM in the XZ plane (
G
X Z
). Starting from 17.7°C, SM XZ was approximately 2482.49MPa. At 31.9°C, the value dropped to around 1611.76MPa, showing a significant decrease of approximately 33% over the studied temperature range. For all the mechanical parameters evaluated, a consistent trend of decrease with increasing temperature is observed. This trend aligns well with known thermal behavior of materials, wherein an increase in temperature typically leads to a reduction in material stiffness [18].

Our investigation has led to several findings concerning the temperature-dependent orthotropic mechanical parameters of a lithium-ion battery. An apparent trend that emerged from our model’s output was a decrease in both the Young’s Modulus and Shear Modulus as the temperature increased from 17.7°C to 31.9°C. The relatively narrow temperature range of this study, as detailed in Section 2, is attributed to the limitations of our experimental setup, particularly the thermal insulation and cooling capacity of the chamber. To begin with, the YM values
E
X
and
E
Z
demonstrated a notable temperature sensitivity. For instance, at 17.7°C, the model produced values for
E
X
3225.49MPa and
E
Z
16 385.65MPa, which were in the same range as those reported by [38], where a YM of 3200MPa was determined for an active pouch cell. Our model evaluated the entire battery cell, including rigid current collectors, in contrast to several literature sources that focused on single cell components, such as separators or electrode sheets. This distinction may account for the discrepancy in our higher
E
Z
values. Additionally, some literature sources reported homogenized or effective YM-values, which typically underrepresent the actual moduli of stiffer cell components. This aspect could further explain why our model’s outputs at 31.9°C (
E
X
: 1828.49MPa) were significantly larger than the effective moduli reported by [39] and [11]. Regarding the SM (
G
X Z
), a similar temperature-dependent decreasing trend was observed. Our study advances the understanding of temperature-dependent mechanical properties in lithium-ion batteries by providing characterizations of the shear modulus. To the best of our knowledge, these values are among the first to be reported for this temperature range at the cell level, offering new insights where previous literature is sparse. While we must acknowledge the possibility of unpublished or lesser-known studies, our results stand to contribute substantially to the body of knowledge on the mechanical behavior of battery materials under thermal influence. These findings invite further investigation and verification, potentially setting a new direction for future research in the field. As discussed in Section 2, the Poisson’s ratio was not included in our model’s output.

Based on the vibration profiles defined in standards such as SAE J2380 and IEC 62660-2, typical automotive applications subject lithium-ion batteries to vibrational loads in the range of
1
0
1
to low
1
0
3
Hz
. These frequencies are critical as they correspond to the operational environment of vehicles, which includes rough road surfaces and other sources of mechanical agitation [24,56,57]. Given this context, our study primarily focused on analyzing the modal characteristics of lithium-ion batteries within this frequency range. While a detailed analysis of the impact of individual vibration modes was outside the scope of our work, it is worth noting that typical applications involve relatively low-frequency excitations [24]. Consequently, the mode with the lowest frequency (mode 1) is assumed to be the most relevant, given its higher deformation characteristics and greater likelihood of being excited during the battery’s operational life. Future studies should consider expanding the model to include mechanical stresses, providing a more comprehensive understanding of the impact of individual modes on battery performance and durability. These findings align with existing standards and literature, underscoring the importance of mode 1 in the operational life of lithium-ion batteries in automotive applications. The limitations of our current study, including the thermal insulation and cooling capacity of the chamber, constrained our ability to explore a broader temperature range. However, this study lays the groundwork for future research, which should aim to refine these models and include experimental validation to enhance the robustness of these findings.

Our model has demonstrated a reasonable capability in capturing the temperature-dependent in-situ behavior of the orthotropic mechanical parameters, aligning fairly with literature values. However, direct experimental validation of these parameters is particularly challenging due to their orthotropic nature. Future work will involve extensive experimental testing, such as in-situ tensile tests as demonstrated by Keshavarzi et al. in [32], which could add valuable insights into the through-plane modulus of the cells. Combining the two methods could lead to a holistic in-situ determination of the mechanical properties of LIB pouch cells under various operating conditions.

This study makes a significant contribution to the field of lithium-ion pouch cell mechanics, providing valuable insights into the temperature-dependency of their mechanical parameters. Our use of EMA and FEM-modeling allowed us to construct an optimized model, closely mirroring the experimental observations. The key findings of our research are:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Our FEM model demonstrated a close match with our experimental findings, validating our approach of parameterizing the model using EMA data. This lays a solid foundation for future work employing similar methodologies.

Our study enhances the current understanding of lithium-ion pouch cell mechanics by presenting an analysis across a relevant temperature spectrum. The discernible temperature dependency of mechanical parameters highlighted by our research underlines the importance of considering the full operational thermal range of these batteries. While previous studies have contributed valuable insights, particularly regarding the impact of state-of-charge, our findings complement and extend this knowledge by shedding light on thermal influences, offering a more comprehensive perspective on the mechanical behavior of pouch cells under varying temperatures.

Our study not only supports findings from existing literature but also introduces additional mechanical parameters, such as the shear modulus, which enhances the depth of our analysis. This contribution provides a more nuanced understanding of the mechanical properties of lithium-ion pouch cells, highlighting the versatility and depth of our investigative approach. The consistency of our findings with those previously reported underscores the reliability of our methodology, reflecting a thoughtful expansion of the established body of knowledge.

ce:section.ce:para: These results bear important implications for the design and optimization of lithium-ion batteries. For instance, considering temperature effects can potentially improve the safety and performance of batteries, especially in settings where the thermal environment is a critical factor. Future work could build upon our study by incorporating other aspects such as aging. The broad applicability of our methodology also suggests that it could be adapted to other types of cells, extending its relevance and utility in the field.

This study provides a novel method for evaluating the mechanical properties of lithium-ion batteries at various temperatures in a non-destructive manner, which is challenging with many other methods. The derived parameters can refine mechanical models, leading to more accurate simulations and a better understanding of battery behavior under thermal stress. These advancements are critical for developing more robust and reliable batteries. Our findings have significant implications for improving the design, safety, and performance of lithium-ion batteries across diverse applications, from electric vehicles to portable electronics. By enhancing the accuracy of finite element models, our work supports the creation of safer, more efficient energy storage solutions.

Our approach, which facilitates the measurement of experimental modal analysis during the cycling of the cell, is particularly relevant when considering the mode mixity factors that are critical in understanding the mechanical behavior of lithium-ion batteries under operational conditions. Although the primary focus of this paper is not on mode mixity factors, our model’s underlying principles are robust and versatile enough to be adapted to assess these factors in various cell geometries, such as those we have studied previously [25,26]. Adapting our model to include the assessment of mode mixity factors in different cell geometries, such as prismatic cells, would require additional development, particularly in creating accurate geometries for the Finite Element Method (FEM) model. This adaptation is crucial for a comprehensive understanding of the mechanical stresses and strains that batteries undergo during different states of charge and operational conditions. Integrating the concept of mode mixity factors into our model, and applying it to the data from these diverse cell geometries, opens up new avenues for research. It not only showcases the flexibility of our model but also significantly contributes to the field by providing a more detailed and practical understanding of the mechanical behavior of lithium-ion batteries. Such insights are invaluable for optimizing battery design and enhancing their performance and longevity in real-world applications.
